experience: Eating at the 'tavola calda' counter of a Palermo friggitoria during the lunch rush, specifically ordering pani ca meusa (spleen sandwich with ricotta and caciocavallo) from one of the handful of remaining vastedde vendors in the Ballarò or Vucciria markets, who prepare it in a cauldron of lard in front of you
where: Ballarò market or Vucciria market, Palermo, Sicily
when: Midday, any day; vendors begin around 10am and sell out by early afternoon
why_distinctive: Pani ca meusa is a hyper-local street food with medieval Arab-Norman roots and was historically the food of Palermo's Jewish quarter. Most food tourists eat arancine; the spleen sandwich requires deliberate seeking. The ritual of ordering 'maritata' (married, with cheese) vs 'schietta' (single, without) is a local distinction almost no visitor knows to make.
source: Documented extensively in Giorgio Locatelli, 'Made in Sicily' (2011); also in Elizabeth Minchilli's 'Eating Rome' blog series on Sicilian street food (2014), and John Irving's long-form piece for Lucky Peach on Palermo street food

pass2_verification.concerns: Three separate sources are cited, which dilutes accountability. Giorgio Locatelli's 'Made in Sicily' (2011) is a real, recognized book by a real author and plausibly covers pani ca meusa as Sicilian street food — this part is credible. However, Elizabeth Minchilli's blog 'Eating Rome' is focused on Rome, not Sicily, making a 'blog series on Sicilian street food' an odd characterization — her blog is Rome-centric by design, though she has written about other Italian regions. The third source — a John Irving piece for Lucky Peach on Palermo street food — is highly suspect: John Irving is primarily known as a novelist (The World According to Garp, etc.), not a food journalist, and no such Lucky Peach piece is recognizable. Lucky Peach did publish long-form food writing but attributing a Palermo street food piece to novelist John Irving is implausible and appears fabricated. The claim itself about pani ca meusa, vastedde vendors, lard cauldrons, Ballarò and Vucciria markets is culturally accurate and well-documented independently, but the sourcing is unreliable due to the likely fabricated Lucky Peach attribution and the mischaracterization of Minchilli's blog.
